sections.header: Verses 1–13 — “Woe to the Drunkards of Ephraim”

heb.paragraph: "Woe (hōy) to that wreath, the pride of Ephraim’s drunkards, to the fading flower, his glorious beauty" (v.1). The first of six woe oracles (chs.28–33). Ephraim’s leaders are drunk; even priests and prophets stagger from beer (v.7). Tables covered with vomit (v.8). They mock Isaiah’s teaching as childish: "Do this, do that, a rule for this, a rule for that, a little here, a little there" (v.10) — the Hebrew (ṣav lāṣav, qav lāqav) mimics baby-talk. God responds: since you treat my word as baby-talk, I will speak to you through foreign lips — the Assyrians (v.11). Paul quotes v.11 in 1 Corinthians 14:21 for the gift of tongues.

notes.note: "Woe to the pride of Ephraim’s drunkards." The fading flower: Samaria’s crown-like position on its hill, wilting under judgment.
"Priests and prophets stagger from beer. All the tables are covered with vomit." Religious leaders drunk on duty. The imagery is deliberately revolting.
"Ṣav lāṣav, qav lāqav." They mock Isaiah as speaking baby-talk. God says: fine — you’ll hear foreign speech you can’t understand at all. Paul quotes (1 Cor 14:21).

refs.note: Amos condemns the same complacent luxury in Samaria — wine and feasting while disaster approaches.
Peter quotes Isaiah 28:16 — Christ as the cornerstone in Zion.
Paul combines Isaiah 28:16 with 8:14 — the stone of stumbling and cornerstone.
Christ as the cornerstone of God's household — building on Isaiah's foundation image.

hist.context: Chapter 28 shifts to 'woe oracles' against Judah, though it begins with Ephraim (northern Israel). The 'fading flower' (v.1) is Samaria's crown — about to fall to Assyria in 722 BC. But the warning extends to Judah: 'these also' (v.7) stagger with wine. The 'cornerstone in Zion' (v.16) promises stability amidst political chaos. Jerusalem's leaders made a 'covenant with death' (v.15), probably alliance with Egypt — trusting human politics rather than divine promise.

notes.note: Oswalt reads Samaria's fall as warning to Judah. The 'fading flower' imagery evokes false beauty about to wither. But verse 5 pivots: Yahweh himself will become a 'glorious crown' for the remnant.
The cornerstone passage is programmatic: God lays a foundation in Zion; those who trust it will not be shaken. Oswalt sees this as the alternative to political alliances — trust in Yahweh's purposes.

Childs notes the structural parallel: Ephraim's judgment (vv.1-4) leads to remnant hope (vv.5-6), then Judah's parallel failure (vv.7-13). The pattern shows Judah learning nothing from Ephraim's example.
Childs emphasizes the cornerstone's canonical function: it provides the criterion by which all other foundations are tested. The NT application to Christ is legitimate extension of Isaiah's meaning.

sections.header: Verses 14–29 — “I Lay in Zion a Stone, a Tested Stone, a Precious Cornerstone”

heb.paragraph: "See, I lay in Zion a stone, a tested stone, a precious cornerstone (pinnâ) for a sure foundation; the one who relies on it will never be stricken with panic" (v.16). Jerusalem’s rulers boast of a "covenant with death" (v.15) — an alliance they think protects them. God says their refuge of lies will be swept away (v.17). Instead, God lays his own foundation: the cornerstone. Peter applies this to Christ (1 Pet 2:6). Paul does too (Rom 9:33; 10:11; Eph 2:20). "I will make justice the measuring line and righteousness the plumb line" (v.17). The farmer parable closes the chapter: God’s discipline is skilled, varied, purposeful — not random violence (vv.23–29).

notes.note: "We have made a covenant with death. We have made a lie our refuge." Then: "I lay in Zion a tested stone, a precious cornerstone." False security versus God’s foundation. Death-covenant versus cornerstone. Every NT author who quotes this passage applies the stone to Christ.
"I will make justice the measuring line and righteousness the plumb line." God builds with moral precision. The tools of construction are ethical, not architectural.

"Does the farmer plough continually? Does he keep on breaking up the soil? No — he plants, threshes gently, varies his method." God as skilled farmer: each crop is handled differently. Discipline is calibrated, not indiscriminate. "All this comes from the LORD Almighty, whose plan is wonderful, whose wisdom is magnificent."

refs.note: Paul quotes Isaiah 28:11-12 regarding tongues — foreign speech as judgment sign.
The earlier 'stone of stumbling' passage that Paul combines with 28:16.
The rejected stone becoming the cornerstone — Jesus applies similar stone imagery.

hist.context: The 'covenant with death' (v.15) is likely Judah's alliance with Egypt against Assyria. Isaiah dismisses it as 'refuge of lies.' When the 'overwhelming scourge' (Assyria) passes through, the alliance will fail. The 'strange work' (v.21) refers to God fighting against his own people — as at Perazim and Gibeon, but now Jerusalem is the enemy. The agricultural parable (vv.23-29) ends on grace: the farmer doesn't thresh endlessly; judgment has limits.

notes.note: Oswalt unpacks the political context: Judah thought alliance with Egypt secured them against Assyria. Isaiah calls it alliance with Death and Sheol — personified powers. Only Yahweh's cornerstone provides true security.

The farming parable is crucial: different crops require different treatment; judgment is proportionate and purposeful. Oswalt sees this as answering the question: will God destroy completely? No — his 'strange work' serves restoration.
Childs notes the irony: leaders think they've secured safety, but their 'covenant with death' will be annulled. Only the cornerstone in Zion — which they dismiss — provides lasting refuge.

Childs sees the farmer parable as hermeneutical key: understanding God requires wisdom. His actions appear confusing but have purpose. The 'wonderful counsel' (v.29) is the same attribute given to the Messiah in 9:6.

ppl.text: "All this comes from the LORD Almighty, whose plan is wonderful, whose wisdom is magnificent…

ppl.name: king
ppl.text: " They mock Isaiah as speaking baby-talk…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVWoe to that wreath, the pride of Ephraim’s drunkards, to the fading flower, his glorious beauty, set on the head of a fertile valley— to that city, the pride of those laid low by wine!ESVAh, the proud crown of the drunkards of Ephraim, and the fading flower of its glorious beauty, which is on the head of the rich valley of those overcome with wine!NIVHe will be a spirit of justice to the one who sits in judgment, a source of strength to those who turn back the battle at the gate.ESVand a spirit of justice to him who sits in judgment, and strength to those who turn back the battle at the gate.

rec.text: ""Woe to the pride of Ephraim’s drunkards." The fading flower: Samaria’s crown-like position on its hill, wilting under judgment."

rows.label: Drunken Leaders — 28:1–13
rows.text: "Woe!" Priests stagger. Tables covered with vomit. Baby-talk mockery. Foreign lips (1 Cor 14:21).

hebtext.note: "Woe (hōy) to that wreath, the pride of Ephraim’s drunkards, to the fading flower, his glorious beauty" (v.1). The first of six woe oracles (chs.28–33). Ephraim’s leaders are drunk; even priests and prophets stagger from beer (v.7). Tables covered with vomit (v.8). They mock Isaiah’s teaching as childish: "Do this, do that, a rule for this, a rule for that, a little here, a little there" (v.10) — the Hebrew (ṣav lāṣav, qav lāqav) mimics baby-talk. God responds: since you treat my word as baby-talk, I will speak to you through foreign lips — the Assyrians (v.11). Paul quotes v.11 in 1 Corinthians 14:21 for the gift of tongues.

"See, I lay in Zion a stone, a tested stone, a precious cornerstone (pinnâ) for a sure foundation; the one who relies on it will never be stricken with panic" (v.16). Jerusalem’s rulers boast of a "covenant with death" (v.15) — an alliance they think protects them. God says their refuge of lies will be swept away (v.17). Instead, God lays his own foundation: the cornerstone. Peter applies this to Christ (1 Pet 2:6). Paul does too (Rom 9:33; 10:11; Eph 2:20). "I will make justice the measuring line and righteousness the plumb line" (v.17). The farmer parable closes the chapter: God’s discipline is skilled, varied, purposeful — not random violence (vv.23–29).

themes.note: Prophecy peaks: the cornerstone (1 Pet 2:6; Rom 9:33; Eph 2:20). Faith: relying on the stone = no panic. Justice: measuring line and plumb line. God the skilled farmer.

coaching.tip: The drunken priests who 'stagger when rendering decisions' (v. 7) reject Isaiah's teaching as baby talk: 'tsav latsav, tsav latsav, qav laqav' (v. 10) — meaningless syllables mocking Isaiah's repetitive warnings. Isaiah fires back: if they won't hear God's clear Hebrew, they'll hear Assyrian — a language they can't understand at all (v. 11). God meets mockery with irony.

'I lay in Zion a stone, a tested stone, a precious cornerstone for a sure foundation' (v. 16). Peter quotes this in 1 Peter 2:6 as christological prophecy. Paul uses it in Romans 9:33. Jesus alludes to it in Matthew 21:42. One verse from an oracle against drunken Ephraim becomes the New Testament's primary image for Christ as foundation. Isaiah buries treasure in unexpected places.
